htmlContent:
I didn't expect fullness to feel like drowning

The first time I noticed it, I was sitting at a dinner table with people I loved. The food was good. The company was warm. And somewhere around the third bite past satisfied, my chest started to tighten. Not from indigestion. From something older. Something that knew this feeling, this fullness, meant danger.

I'm not talking about overeating at Thanksgiving. I'm talking about the moment your stomach sends the signal that you've had enough. And instead of feeling content, you feel exposed. Vulnerable. Like you've done something wrong just by being full.

Here's the thing. I spent years thinking this was about food. About calories. About discipline. But the more I learned about how the nervous system actually works, the more I realized that fullness itself can activate shame. Not because of what you ate. But because of what fullness meant in your body's history.

Stay with me here. Because this is where it gets weird and specific and maybe a little uncomfortable.

Fullness is a sensation of expansion. Your stomach stretches. Your diaphragm presses upward. Your ribs widen slightly. For most people with secure attachment and safe developmental histories, this feels like satisfaction. Comfort. A signal that needs have been met.

But for those of us who grew up in homes where having needs was dangerous, where taking up space meant punishment, where being seen as wanting something meant shame, fullness can feel like a threat. It activates the same protective responses that kept you small and invisible as a child.

Think about it. When you're full, you're harder to ignore. Your body becomes more present. More solid. More here. And if being here was never safe, fullness becomes something to escape from.

The body remembers what the mind forgets

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," spent decades showing us that trauma lives in the body's tissues, not just in the memories we can access with words. He documented how the physical sensations of safety and threat get coded into our nervous systems before we can even form sentences about them.

So when you feel full, your body doesn't just process stomach distension. It scans for context. Is this safe? Am I allowed to be this size right now? Will taking up this space get me hurt? And if your history says no, your nervous system will generate shame as a survival strategy. Shame makes you small. Shame makes you want to disappear. Shame is the body's way of saying, "Quick, become invisible before someone notices you exist."

Wild, right? Your own fullness becomes a trigger for the very response that once kept you alive.

I remember reading van der Kolk's description of how the vagus nerve connects the gut to the brain and realizing that the sensation of fullness travels the exact same neural pathways as the sensation of being trapped. The vagus nerve doesn't know the difference between a full stomach and a tight chest from fear. It just knows that something is pressing upward, inward, and it sends that signal to the brainstem. The brainstem doesn't think. It reacts.

So here's what happens. You eat a normal meal. Your stomach fills. The vagus nerve sends the signal. Your brainstem interprets the pressure as a potential threat. And your brain, trying to make sense of a threat that has no obvious source, generates shame. Because shame is the emotion that explains danger when the danger is you.

Notice what just happened in your body reading that. If you felt a tightening somewhere, you're not alone. This is real. And it's not your fault.

Shame as a protective strategy against being seen

Pat Ogden, a pioneer in sensorimotor psychotherapy, talks about how the body organizes itself around survival. When you were a child and needed to be small to stay safe, your body learned to collapse inward. Shoulders forward. Chest hollow. Stomach tight. Breath shallow. This posture says, "I'm not a threat. Please don't notice me. I'll take up as little space as possible."

Fullness directly opposes this posture. A full stomach pushes your diaphragm down. Your chest has to open slightly to accommodate the food. Your shoulders might even roll back without you realizing it. Your body is literally expanding into the space around you. And if your nervous system associates expansion with danger, it will fight back.

That fight back feels like shame. A wave of heat. A desire to curl up. A sudden critical voice in your head saying you ate too much, you're greedy, you don't deserve this. That voice isn't truth. It's protection. It's your nervous system trying to get you to collapse back into safety, back into invisibility, back into the posture that kept you alive when you were small.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium glycinate specifically supports the nervous system's ability to down-regulate after activation. It won't fix the shame response on its own, but it can help your body stay slightly more grounded when those fullness sensations arise. I take it most nights and it helps my system settle.

But supplements are just support. The real work is understanding that the shame isn't about the food. It's about the history your body carries of what it meant to take up space.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It directly addresses how growing up with parents who couldn't handle your needs teaches you to make yourself small. Reading it helped me understand why fullness felt so dangerous. Because being full meant I had a need. And having needs in that household was not allowed.

The spiral of shame and restriction

Here's where it gets complicated. The shame from fullness doesn't just sit there. It creates action. It tells you to fix the feeling, to get rid of the fullness, to return to the safe state of emptiness. For some people, that means restriction. For others, it means purging. For many, it means a constant negotiation with food that has nothing to do with hunger or nutrition.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, would say that the shame response to fullness is a thwarted orienting response. Your body wants to move away from the sensation. But you can't move away from a sensation inside you. So the energy gets stuck. It loops. It creates the feeling of being trapped in your own body.

And trapped bodies generate shame. Because shame is the emotion that says, "This is my fault. If I were different, this wouldn't be happening. I am the problem." It's a way for the nervous system to regain a sense of control. If the problem is me, then maybe I can fix myself. If the problem is outside me, I'm helpless.

I've sat with clients who described feeling shame not just after eating, but during. The moment they felt the first sensation of fullness, the shame would arrive like a reflex. Faster than thought. Faster than any cognitive reframe. And no amount of "intuitive eating" affirmations could touch it, because the shame wasn't in the thinking brain. It was in the body's memory of what happened the last time they were visible.

If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You (paid link). Mark Wolynn's work on inherited family trauma explores how the shame and survival responses of your ancestors can live in your nervous system. Sometimes the fullness shame isn't even yours. It's your mother's shame. Your grandmother's. The lineage of women who learned that having needs was dangerous and passed that lesson down in their cells.

For those looking for gentle nervous system support, something worth trying is NOW L-Theanine 200mg (paid link). L-theanine promotes alpha brain waves, which are associated with a calm but alert state. It can help take the edge off the shame response without dulling your awareness. I use it when I know I'm going into a situation where fullness might trigger me, like a holiday meal or a dinner with family.

What to do when fullness activates shame

I'm not going to give you a five-step plan to fix this. That would be dishonest. This is deep work. It takes time. But there are things you can try that might help your nervous system start to associate fullness with safety instead of danger.

First, slow down. Way down. The shame response is fast. It happens before you can think. But if you slow your eating to a pace where you can notice the first hint of fullness, before it becomes overwhelming, you give your nervous system a chance to orient. You can feel the sensation and say, "Oh, that's what full feels like. I'm still safe."

Second, name the shame out loud. "I'm feeling shame right now. This is old. This is not about this meal." Something happens when you verbalize it. The prefrontal cortex comes online. The limbic system calms slightly. You shift from being inside the shame to observing it.

Third, move. Not to burn calories. To complete the thwarted escape response. Stand up. Stretch. Walk around the block. Shake your hands. Your body wanted to flee from the fullness sensation. Let it move now. Let the energy discharge. Levine calls this pendulation. Moving between the uncomfortable sensation and a resource. The resource can be anything. A safe memory. A breathing rhythm. The feeling of your feet on the floor.

Fourth, breathe into the fullness. This sounds counterintuitive. When you're full and ashamed, you probably want to breathe shallowly, to make yourself smaller. Try the opposite. Breathe into your belly. Let the fullness be there. Let your ribs expand. Your nervous system needs to learn that expansion is not death. It needs practice staying open while full.

And fifth, get curious. Ask your body, "What do you need right now?" Not what the shame voice says you should do. What does your body actually need? Maybe it needs to be touched gently. Maybe it needs warmth. Maybe it needs to be told, "You're allowed to be full. You're allowed to take up space. I'm not going anywhere."

This work is not linear. Some days you'll eat a normal meal and feel nothing but satisfaction. Other days the shame will hit like a wave. That's okay. That's not failure. That's your nervous system doing what it learned to do to keep you alive. You're just teaching it a new way now.

And that teaching happens one meal at a time. One breath at a time. One moment of staying open when everything in you wants to collapse.

You're not broken for feeling shame when you're full. You're human. You're a body that learned to survive. And now you're learning to live.
